content.text: At the core of every modern large language model lies a single operation that changed how neural networks process sequences: scaled dot-product self-attention. Rather than encoding context through recurrence or fixed-size convolution windows, self-attention allows each token in a sequence to directly query every other token, assembling a context-weighted summary in one parallelizable step. This mechanism is the building block that makes transformers fast, scalable, and remarkably capable at capturing long-range linguistic relationships.
Self-attention is a sequence-to-sequence function that maps an input matrix

(n tokens, each represented by a d-dimensional embedding) to an output matrix of the same shape, where each output row is a context-aware summary of the entire sequence as seen from that token's perspective.

The mechanism works by projecting the input into three derived spaces using learned weight matrices: queries (Q), keys (K), and values (V). Queries represent what each position is looking for; keys represent what each position offers; values carry the actual information to be aggregated. A dot product between every query and every key produces a raw compatibility score, which is scaled, passed through softmax to yield normalized attention weights, and then used to take a weighted combination of the values.
is the key dimension. The divisor
is a stabilizing scale factor that prevents the dot products from growing so large in magnitude that the softmax gradients vanish.

Before transformers, sequence models relied on RNNs and LSTMs to propagate context from one token to the next through a hidden state. This recurrent bottleneck created two deep problems: (1) information from distant tokens had to flow through many sequential steps, making it easy for gradients to vanish and for context to be lost, and (2) sequential processing precluded parallelization, making training on long sequences extremely slow.

Self-attention eliminates both problems. Every token attends directly to every other token in a single matrix operation, so the path length between any two tokens is always one — there is no information highway that must be traversed step by step. And because the computation is expressed entirely in terms of matrix multiplications, it maps directly onto modern GPU/TPU hardware that is designed to parallelize exactly this class of operation.

Beyond performance, self-attention is also more interpretable than a black-box hidden state. The attention weight matrix explicitly shows which tokens each position found relevant, a property used extensively in mechanistic interpretability research to understand what language models have learned.
Any token can attend to any other token with equal computational cost regardless of distance. A verb at the end of a long clause can attend directly to its subject at the beginning.
All query-key comparisons happen simultaneously as a single batch of matrix multiplications. Training scales to tens of thousands of tokens on modern hardware.
Attention weights are input-dependent and continuous, so the model learns exactly how much to weight each source token for each target position and task.

By running multiple attention heads in parallel with different projections, the model can simultaneously represent syntactic dependencies, coreference, semantic similarity, and other distinct types of relational information.
Attention weights expose explicit token-to-token relevance scores that can be visualized and analyzed.
Self-attention representations pre-trained on large corpora transfer remarkably well to downstream tasks with minimal fine-tuning, as demonstrated by BERT, GPT, and their successors.
memory where n is the sequence length. A 4 096-token context needs 16M attention scores per head per layer — manageable, but a 100k-token context becomes expensive.

Self-attention is permutation-equivariant: it treats the sequence as an unordered set. Position information must be injected separately via positional encodings (sinusoidal, learned, RoPE, ALiBi, etc.).
Storing all intermediate activations (attention scores, weights, values) during training requires significantly more memory than an equivalent RNN.
For highly local tasks like pixel-level image processing, the global all-pairs comparison is wasteful compared to convolutions that exploit spatial locality.
Standard self-attention cannot extend beyond its training context length without architectural modifications (e.g. sliding window attention, RoPE extension, or retrieval augmentation).
Start with an input sequence of n tokens, each represented as a
. The attention head projects X into three different subspaces using learned matrices

attrs.latex: Q = XW_Q, \quad K = XW_K, \quad V = XW_V

content.text: produces a score matrix where entry
measures how compatible query token i is with key token j. Dividing by
normalizes the magnitude: without this scaling, scores grow proportional to
, pushing the softmax into saturation where gradients become vanishingly small.
Applying row-wise softmax converts the raw scores into a proper probability distribution (each row sums to 1), yielding the attention weight matrix
. The output is the weighted sum of value vectors:
In practice, transformers use multi-head attention (MHA), which runs h independent attention heads with smaller projections
, then concatenates the results and applies a final linear projection
. Each head learns to focus on a different relational pattern. With
, each head operates in a 64-dimensional subspace — a common configuration in the original transformer.

The diagram below shows the data flow through one scaled dot-product self-attention head, from the input matrix X through the Q, K, V projections, the score computation, softmax normalization, and the final weighted sum:
In the complete transformer encoder, this attention block is followed by a residual connection and layer normalization (
), then a position-wise feed-forward network with another residual + layer norm. Multiple such encoder layers are stacked to progressively build deeper contextual representations.
Sentence classification, named entity recognition, machine translation, summarization, question answering — any task where tokens must reference other tokens across the sequence.
Any sequence where meaningful relationships exist between distant elements: code understanding, document classification, genome sequence analysis.
When training a general-purpose encoder or decoder on a large corpus for later fine-tuning.
Vision transformers (ViT) apply self-attention to image patches; cross-modal transformers attend between text and image tokens.
Standard self-attention becomes prohibitively expensive. Use sparse attention (Longformer, BigBird), linear attention, or state-space models (Mamba).
Image segmentation at pixel level benefits from convolutions that exploit spatial locality at lower cost.
The quadratic memory footprint makes on-device long-context inference expensive; consider RNN-based or recurrent-hybrid architectures.
Transformers are data-hungry; for datasets with a few thousand examples and structured features, gradient-boosted trees or shallow networks may outperform.
Implementing self-attention from first principles takes four steps:
Apply three linear transformations to the input:
Matrix multiply Q by the transpose of K and divide by
Add -inf to masked positions before softmax (causal mask for autoregressive decoding, padding mask to ignore PAD tokens).
Normalize scores row-wise with softmax, then multiply the resulting weight matrix by V.
For production use, multi-head attention wraps this computation with head splitting and merging, and PyTorch provides a fused kernel via
(PyTorch 2.0+) that dispatches to FlashAttention on CUDA when available.

import numpy as np

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    """
    Compute scaled dot-product attention.

    Args:
        Q: query matrix, shape (batch, seq_q, d_k)
        K: key matrix,   shape (batch, seq_k, d_k)
        V: value matrix, shape (batch, seq_k, d_v)
        mask: optional boolean mask, shape (batch, seq_q, seq_k)
              True entries are masked out (set to -inf before softmax)

    Returns:
        output: shape (batch, seq_q, d_v)
        weights: attention weights, shape (batch, seq_q, seq_k)
    """
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    # Step 1: compute raw scores — (batch, seq_q, seq_k)
    scores = np.matmul(Q, K.transpose(0, 2, 1)) / np.sqrt(d_k)

    # Step 2: apply mask (e.g. causal mask or padding mask)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = np.where(mask, -1e9, scores)

    # Step 3: softmax over the key dimension
    scores_max = scores.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True)  # numerical stability
    exp_scores = np.exp(scores - scores_max)
    weights = exp_scores / exp_scores.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True)

    # Step 4: weighted sum of values
    output = np.matmul(weights, V)
    return output, weights


# --- Minimal demonstration ---
np.random.seed(42)
batch, seq_len, d_k, d_v = 2, 5, 16, 16

Q = np.random.randn(batch, seq_len, d_k).astype(np.float32)
K = np.random.randn(batch, seq_len, d_k).astype(np.float32)
V = np.random.randn(batch, seq_len, d_v).astype(np.float32)

output, weights = scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V)
print(f"Output shape:  {output.shape}")   # (2, 5, 16)
print(f"Weights shape: {weights.shape}")  # (2, 5, 5)
print(f"Weights row sum: {weights[0, 0].sum():.4f}")  # ~1.0

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


class MultiHeadSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Multi-Head Self-Attention as described in "Attention Is All You Need".

    Each head independently projects Q, K, V to a lower-dimensional space,
    computes scaled dot-product attention, then concatenates all heads and
    projects back with W_O.
    """

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0, "d_model must be divisible by num_heads"

        self.d_model = d_model
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads  # dimension per head

        # Fused projection matrices: one linear for all heads at once
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self._init_weights()

    def _init_weights(self):
        for w in [self.W_q, self.W_k, self.W_v, self.W_o]:
            nn.init.xavier_uniform_(w.weight)

    def split_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Reshape (B, S, d_model) → (B, H, S, d_k)."""
        B, S, _ = x.shape
        return x.view(B, S, self.num_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def merge_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Reshape (B, H, S, d_k) → (B, S, d_model)."""
        B, H, S, _ = x.shape
        return x.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, S, self.d_model)

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,
        mask: torch.Tensor | None = None,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Args:
            x:    (B, S, d_model)
            mask: (B, 1, S, S) boolean, True = masked position
        Returns:
            (B, S, d_model)
        """
        Q = self.split_heads(self.W_q(x))   # (B, H, S, d_k)
        K = self.split_heads(self.W_k(x))
        V = self.split_heads(self.W_v(x))

        scale = math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / scale  # (B, H, S, S)

        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))

        attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        attn_weights = self.dropout(attn_weights)

        context = torch.matmul(attn_weights, V)       # (B, H, S, d_k)
        context = self.merge_heads(context)            # (B, S, d_model)
        return self.W_o(context)


# --- Quick smoke test ---
B, S, d_model, num_heads = 4, 32, 256, 8
mha = MultiHeadSelfAttention(d_model, num_heads)
x = torch.randn(B, S, d_model)
out = mha(x)
print(f"Input:  {x.shape}")   # torch.Size([4, 32, 256])
print(f"Output: {out.shape}") # torch.Size([4, 32, 256])
params = sum(p.numel() for p in mha.parameters())
print(f"Parameters: {params:,}")   # 262,144

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


def causal_attention(Q, K, V):
    """
    Autoregressive (causal) self-attention: position i can only attend
    to positions 0 … i.  Positions i+1, i+2, … are masked out.
    """
    B, H, S, d_k = Q.shape

    # Upper-triangular mask: True = future token = masked out
    causal = torch.ones(S, S, dtype=torch.bool).triu(diagonal=1)  # (S, S)
    causal = causal.unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)                       # (1, 1, S, S)

    scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)
    scores = scores.masked_fill(causal, float("-inf"))

    weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)          # (B, H, S, S)
    # NaN guard: positions where all keys are masked become NaN after softmax
    weights = torch.nan_to_num(weights, nan=0.0)

    return torch.matmul(weights, V)


def inspect_causal_mask(seq_len: int = 6):
    """Print the causal attention weight pattern for a short sequence."""
    d_k = 32
    Q = torch.randn(1, 1, seq_len, d_k)
    K = torch.randn(1, 1, seq_len, d_k)
    V = torch.randn(1, 1, seq_len, d_k)
    out = causal_attention(Q, K, V)

    # Build and display the mask
    mask = torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len).tril()  # 1 = allowed, 0 = blocked
    print("Causal mask (1=attend, 0=blocked):")
    for row in mask.tolist():
        print("  " + "  ".join(str(int(v)) for v in row))
    print(f"Output shape: {out.shape}")  # (1, 1, 6, 32)


inspect_causal_mask()


# Using PyTorch's built-in scaled_dot_product_attention (torch >= 2.0)
# This dispatches to FlashAttention on CUDA when available.
Q = torch.randn(4, 8, 128, 64)  # (batch, heads, seq, d_k)
K = torch.randn(4, 8, 128, 64)
V = torch.randn(4, 8, 128, 64)

# is_causal=True generates the causal mask internally — no manual masking needed
out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True)
print(f"FlashAttention output: {out.shape}")  # (4, 8, 128, 64)

BERT-base-uncased uses 12 transformer encoder layers, each with 12 attention heads and a model dimension of 768 (so each head operates in 64 dimensions). When processing the sentence "The bank can guarantee deposits will eventually cover future tuition costs", the word "bank" must resolve its ambiguous meaning. Self-attention in the later layers learns to assign high attention weights from "bank" to "deposits" and "cover" — financial context words — rather than to river-related words, disambiguating the sense without any task-specific rules.
The snippet below loads BERT and inspects its attention patterns:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

# Load a pre-trained BERT model — its encoder is stacked self-attention layers
model_name = "bert-base-uncased"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_name, output_attentions=True)
model.eval()

text = "The transformer architecture relies on self-attention."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# outputs.last_hidden_state: (1, seq_len, 768) — contextualized token embeddings
# outputs.attentions: tuple of 12 tensors, one per layer, each (1, 12, seq_len, seq_len)
hidden = outputs.last_hidden_state
attn_layer_0 = outputs.attentions[0]  # first transformer layer

tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])
seq_len = len(tokens)

print(f"Tokens ({seq_len}): {tokens}")
print(f"Hidden states: {hidden.shape}")        # (1, seq_len, 768)
print(f"Attention (layer 0): {attn_layer_0.shape}")  # (1, 12, seq_len, seq_len)

# Attention from [CLS] token (pos 0) averaged over all 12 heads
cls_attn = attn_layer_0[0].mean(dim=0)[0]  # (seq_len,)
print("\nCLS attention over tokens (layer 0, avg heads):")
for tok, weight in zip(tokens, cls_attn.tolist()):
    bar = "=" * int(weight * 40)
    print(f"  {tok:15s}  {weight:.3f}  {bar}")
The hidden state for each token in

is the contextualized representation the model built by attending across all 12 layers. Downstream task heads (classifiers, span predictors) are then trained on top of these rich representations, which is why BERT and its variants achieve strong results across dozens of NLP benchmarks with remarkably little task-specific data.
divisor causes dot products to grow large in magnitude as
increases, pushing softmax into a saturated regime where gradients are near zero and training stalls. Always scale before softmax.
Applying a mask of 0.0 instead of -inf causes the masked positions to receive non-zero softmax weight (since
in float32) to guarantee masked positions receive zero attention weight.
When splitting d_model into h heads,
must be evenly divisible by h. Failing this check silently produces wrong shapes in the split/merge logic. Always assert
At autoregressive step 0 (the first token), the causal mask blocks all keys, making the softmax input all -inf.
after softmax, or use PyTorch's built-in
Using absolute positions with extended contexts

Standard learned absolute positional embeddings do not generalize to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training. For long-context models, use Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) or ALiBi, which extrapolate or interpolate to unseen lengths more gracefully.
Use Xavier/Glorot initialization for Q, K, V, O weight matrices.
This keeps the scale of projections consistent with the input scale and prevents exploding activations early in training.

Dropout on the attention weight matrix (after softmax, before matmul with V) acts as a form of structural regularization by randomly zeroing out attention connections during training. Typical rates: 0.1.
Modern transformer variants (GPT-2+, LLaMA) apply layer norm before the attention block rather than after (post-norm). Pre-norm produces more stable gradients at large depth.
automatically selects FlashAttention when running on CUDA, reducing both runtime and peak memory with no change in output.
Match head count to model dimension.
A head dimension of 64 is a well-tested default. For d_model = 512 use 8 heads; for d_model = 1024 use 16 heads.
If attention weights collapse to near-one-hot distributions early in training, the model may be under-regularized or the learning rate is too high.
In PyTorch, pass boolean masks to
(True = mask out). Using float masks mixed with boolean logic is a common source of subtle sign errors.
For a sequence of length n and model dimension d:

score matrix. At n = 512 that's 262k entries per head; at n = 8 192 it's 67M entries per head. With 96 heads in GPT-4-class models the memory pressure becomes the primary constraint for long contexts.

FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) rewrites the attention kernel to fuse the score computation, softmax, and value aggregation into a single CUDA kernel that uses tiling to avoid materializing the full
score matrix in GPU HBM (high-bandwidth memory). This reduces memory complexity from
while producing numerically identical results. FlashAttention 2 further improves thread parallelism and is the default backend in PyTorch 2.0+ when CUDA is available.
For very long sequences, sparse attention patterns (e.g. Longformer's sliding window + global tokens, BigBird's random + block + global attention) reduce the cost to
where w is the window size. Linear attention methods (Performer, RWKV) reformulate attention to avoid the softmax entirely, achieving
complexity at the cost of reduced expressiveness.
Production-quality self-attention implementations for BERT, GPT-2/3/4, LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon, and dozens of other architectures. Provides attention maps via
API mirrors PyTorch's interface. Used by Google's PaLM and Gemini training pipelines.
) provides fused forward and backward passes, supporting fp16 and bf16, variable-length sequences, and sliding-window attention.
Meta's library of memory-efficient attention kernels, including block-sparse, memory-efficient attention, and custom CUDA extensions.
API automatically converts standard attention layers to use PyTorch's fused kernel.
makes head-splitting and merging transformations clearer and less error-prone than raw
Self-attention's key advantage is exact, unrestricted global context at the cost of quadratic scaling. Alternatives trade some context quality or flexibility for reduced compute and memory.

Self-attention is susceptible to adversarial token injection. Adding a small number of adversarially crafted tokens to a prompt can dramatically shift attention weights, causing the model to ignore legitimate context and produce attacker-controlled outputs. This is the mechanism behind prompt injection attacks in LLM-based applications. Mitigations include instruction-following fine-tuning, input sanitization, and limiting the attention window available to untrusted input.
Empirically, the first token in a sequence (often a special

token) accumulates disproportionately large attention weight as a "sink" for attention when no other token is highly relevant. This is benign in practice but can distort attention visualization tools into suggesting that the first token is semantically meaningful for every query.
Computing softmax over very large score vectors can overflow in float32. The standard fix is the log-sum-exp trick: subtract the row maximum before exponentiating. PyTorch's
and FlashAttention implement this automatically. If implementing attention from scratch (e.g. in NumPy), always apply the numerical stability correction shown in the examples above.

Deep transformers (96+ layers) can diverge during early training if attention logits grow too large. Pre-norm (layer norm before attention) and careful learning rate warmup schedules are the primary mitigations. Some architectures also apply learnable or fixed temperature scaling to the attention scores.

Think of each token as a researcher at a conference, holding a badge describing what they need (query) and a nametag describing who they are (key). Every researcher scans every other researcher's nametag, computes a compatibility score, and then assembles a personalized summary by taking a weighted average of everyone's elevator pitch (value). Researchers with closely matching needs and identities contribute more to the summary; irrelevant attendees barely register. The entire conference happens simultaneously — all researchers query all others in one pass — which is why self-attention is so fast on parallel hardware.
Self-attention computes a context-weighted summary for every token by scoring it against all other tokens.
Three learned projections — Q, K, V — let the model learn what to query, what to expose as a key, and what information to aggregate.
They are not the same matrix; each learns a different role.
The scaling factor 1/sqrt(d_k) is not optional.
Without it, softmax saturates and gradients vanish as head dimension grows.
Multi-head attention runs h parallel heads on d_model/h dimensions each, then concatenates.
Each head can specialize in a different type of relational pattern.
Self-attention is O(n²) in both time and memory.
This is manageable for sequences under ~8k tokens; FlashAttention reduces peak memory to O(n) using tiling.
Causal masking converts the encoder into an autoregressive decoder.
Setting upper-triangular scores to -inf ensures position i only attends to positions 0…i.
Positional information is not inherent — it must be injected.
Sinusoidal, learned, RoPE, and ALiBi embeddings are the main approaches, each with different extrapolation properties.
FlashAttention (PyTorch 2.0+) is the recommended production backend.
It is numerically identical to naive attention but up to 3× faster and uses O(n) memory.
